coredata.dc:title: Green preparation of palm powder-derived carbon dots co-doped with sulfur/chlorine and their application in visible-light photocatalysis
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Ziqi
Yang, Pei
Li, Xinghui
Luo, Min
Zhang, Wei
Chen, Minzhi
Zhou, Xiaoyan
coredata.dc:description: To exploit photocatalytic performance is one of the worthiest efforts for expanding the application of carbon dots (CDs) owing to their low-cost starting materials, facile preparation and simple operation without secondary pollution. Herein, novel biomass-based CDs (Bio-CDs) were successfully synthesized from waste palm powders by using one-step hydrothermal method under the facilitation of thionyl chloride on carbonization process and doping effect. The resultant Bio-CDs exhibited quasi-spherical shape with an average size of 3.54 nm and displayed blue-emissive fluorescence with excitation-dependent behavior. Benefiting from the S and Cl co-doped structure and small size effect, the synthesized Bio-CDs possessed high photocatalytic activity towards the degradation of organic dyes under visible light. The degradation of the Rhodamine B was nearly 71.7%, while the Methylene Blue was almost decomposed completely (ca. 94.2%), which make the Bio-CDs to be a hopeful candidate as photocatalyst for the reduction of organic pollutants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Palm powders
Photodegradation
Visible light
Dye pollutant

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful to the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 31870549 ), the Jiangsu Nature Science Foundation ( BK20161524 ), the Program for 333 Talents Project in Jiangsu Province (Grant No. BRA2016381 ), the Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province ( KYCX17_839 ), the Advanced Analysis and Testing Center of Nanjing Forestry University .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the synthesis of Bio-CDs. (b) TEM image of Bio-CDs. (c) HR-TEM image of Bio-CDs. (d) The particle size distribution of Bio-CDs. (e) FT-IR spectrum of Bio-CDs. (f) XPS survey spectrum and high-resolution XPS of (g) C 1s, (h) O 1s (i) S 2p and (j) Cl 2p spectra of Bio-CDs.

(a) Emission spectra of Bio-CDs aqueous solution. (b) Normalized PL spectra of Bio-CDs. (c) Excitation and absorption spectra of Bio-CDs in aqueous solution (inset: photographs taken under visible light and 365 nm UV light). (d) Fluorescence decay curves of Bio-CDs, insert is the corresponding biexponential function.

Photocatalytic degradation of (a) RhB and (b) MB in the absence and presence of Bio-CDs under different irradiation conditions at room temperature, respectively. Insets represent the photographs of mixed solutions before and after the photodegradation. The linear fitting based on ln(Ct/C0) = −kt for the degradation reactions of (c) RhB and (d) MB.
Schematic illustration of electronic transition in the Bio-CDs structure.
Contents of various chemical bonds in Bio-CDs as determined by XPS.

head.ce:title: Green preparation of palm powder-derived carbon dots co-doped with sulfur/chlorine and their application in visible-light photocatalysis

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing, 210037, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing, 210037, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing, 210037, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing, 210037, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To exploit photocatalytic performance is one of the worthiest efforts for expanding the application of carbon dots (CDs) owing to their low-cost starting materials, facile preparation and simple operation without secondary pollution. Herein, novel biomass-based CDs (Bio-CDs) were successfully synthesized from waste palm powders by using one-step hydrothermal method under the facilitation of thionyl chloride on carbonization process and doping effect. The resultant Bio-CDs exhibited quasi-spherical shape with an average size of 3.54 nm and displayed blue-emissive fluorescence with excitation-dependent behavior. Benefiting from the S and Cl co-doped structure and small size effect, the synthesized Bio-CDs possessed high photocatalytic activity towards the degradation of organic dyes under visible light. The degradation of the Rhodamine B was nearly 71.7%, while the Methylene Blue was almost decomposed completely (ca. 94.2%), which make the Bio-CDs to be a hopeful candidate as photocatalyst for the reduction of organic pollutants.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted considerable interests since being discovered serendipitously in 2004 [1]. Compared with the traditional semiconductor quantum dots and organic fluorescent dyes, CDs are characterized by multiple and stable color emission, low toxicity, abundant surface functional groups and facile preparation and modification. Because of their numerous marvelous properties, CDs show promise as a photoluminescent (PL) nanomaterial for applications in the electronic, optical, catalytic, biological fields, etc. [2–8].

So far, multiple approaches have been pursued to synthesize CDs, such as laser ablation of graphite, microwave-heating of carbohydrate, hydrothermal carbonization of polymer and plasma pyrolysis of albumen [9–12]. Among these developed synthesis strategies, the facile hydrothermal approach, as the most traditional method, has been extensively confirmed to be an effective way to synthesize CDs from biomass-based materials. In addition to promising industrialization with respect to easy operation, economical set-up and safety of operation, the hydrothermal method can provide efficient dehydration, polymerization and carbonization effects towards the biomass-based materials featuring with complicated chemical composition. Over the past decades, synthesizing of nanomaterials using nontoxic precursor, environmentally solvent and renewable carbon source is the key issue, and which is also being taken into significant considerations for the green synthesis of CDs. Noteworthily, nature provides a limitless carbon sources which meet the requirement of synthesizing CDs contained sp2-conjugated carbon cores and multiple functional groups. Recently, there are increasing researchers have focused on preparing CDs using natural materials, including milk [13], limeade [14], pomelo peel [15], linseed [16], xylan [17], bombyx mori silk [18], xylitol [19] and so on. The most of these synthesized biomass-derived CDs have validated their use in the field of bio-imaging and fluorescent sensing, but their application in photocatalytic degradation of pollutants is very rare. Compared with the application in imaging and sensing requiring slight biomass-based CDs, the photocatalysis is prone to large number of light-catalyzed CDs, which provides a more promising route for value-added utilization of waste biomass.

In recent years, some metal oxides have gained remarkable acceptance as a photocatalyst owing to appropriate band gap, thus making them suitable light-responsive catalysts. Nevertheless, these materials are usually characterized by high surface energy, which can result in adverse aggregation [20,21]. In addition, significant researches have proved that g-C3N4, a burgeoning semiconductor material, possesses distinctive photo-electronic properties. However, the visible-light absorption of pure g-C3N4 is very poor, along with the requirement of suitable electron donor to hinder quick recombination of the photoexcited e−/h+ pairs [22]. For this issue, great efforts have therefore been made to prepare nanocomposites combining CDs with light-catalyzed semiconductors and metal oxide, and the pioneers’ works have also proved that the photocatalytic capacity of the resultant composites can be enhanced significantly by the incorporation of CDs. Nevertheless, there are few research disclose the photocatalysis caused by the CDs alone [23–25]. It is known that a key issue influencing the photocatalytic capability is the nature of surface/interface chemistry of the CDs. According to previous researches, the attractive properties of the CDs are commonly attributed to the quantum-confinement effects, as well as structural defects induced by finite size effects and heteroatom incorporation [5,26,27]. In contrast to the tradition semiconductor quantum dots, which have an explicitly size-dependent band gap, the structural defects play an important role in the band gap formation within the microstructure of CDs [28–30]. Considering that new electronic states in CDs formed by doping heteroatoms have large effect on the recombination and separation of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs, appropriate surface modification is frequently necessary to promote photocatalysis.

Herein, novel S and Cl co-doped CDs were facilely synthesized via one-pot hydrothermal treatment, while wood powders of palm veneers were served as carbon source with thionyl chloride being the dopant. The characterizations for morphology, size distribution and chemical composition of biomass-based CDs (Bio-CDs) were investigated in detail, it was found that the synthesized Bio-CDs were well doped heteroatom within their structures, thus demonstrating the easy functionalization of biomass derived CDs. More importantly, these S, Cl co-doped nanostructures were confirmed to possess outstanding photocatalysis, showing superior photodegradation of organic dyes in visible light, this research will provide a new strategy for sewage disposal and value-added utilization of waste biomass.

Palm powders were obtained by milling palm veneer which was purchased from Malaysia. Thionyl chloride, Rhodamine B (RhB) and Methylene Blue (MB) were procured from Nanjing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Nanjing, China). Purified water (Hangzhou, China) was used in all experiments. All chemical reagents were used as received without further purification.

Bio-CDs were prepared using palm powder as carbon source via a green synthesis process. Firstly, the raw palm veneers were ground into fine powders (<100 mesh), and 0.1 g powders were dispersed in 10 mL of purified water, followed by adding 200 μL thionyl chloride as the dopant. Subsequently, the mixture was put into a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (25 mL) and heated at 200 °C for 7 h. After the reaction cooled down to room temperature completely, the resultant dark yellow solution was filtered using a 0.22 μm syringe filter to remove agglomerated particles and then heated at 95 °C to remove possible residual thionyl chloride. Before further tests, the obtained samples were subjected to dialysis (500 Da of cut off molecular weight) against ultrapure water for 48 h to remove the unreacted small molecules. All solid samples were obtained under vacuum freeze-drying condition prior to further characterization.

The photocatalytic degradation activity of Bio-CDs was evaluated by the degradation of RhB and MB. 20 mL of Bio-CDs were added to aqueous solutions of RhB (20 mL, 30 mg/L) and MB (20 mL, 30 mg/L), respectively. The mixed solutions were stirred for 30 min in the dark to reach the adsorption and desorption equilibration. The solutions were then exposed to different irradiation conditions (visible light or in darkness) to evaluate the photocatalytic activity of Bio-CDs. The total volume of the reaction solution is 40 mL with the initial concentration of RhB and MB being 15 mg/L. A xenon lamp with an optical filter cutting off wavelengths below 420 nm was placed 20 cm away from the entire solution and provided the visible light source with a power density of 400 mW/cm2. During the photocatalytic tests, fixed amounts of the reaction solution were periodically sampled. The quantity of RhB and MB in the collected solutions were determined by UV–vis absorbance spectroscopy at 554 nm and 665 nm, respectively.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images and High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were performed on a JEM-2100 UHR (JEOL, Japan) operating at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a VERTEX 80 V spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) ranging from 500 to 4000 cm−1 with the KBr pellets technique. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was obtained on the AXIS UltraDLD spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The solar light was generated by a solar simulator (CEL HXF300, CEAULIGHT, Beijing, China). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption of Bio-CDs solution was measured on Lambda 950 (PE, American). The photoluminescence (PL) and photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra were carried out with an F-7000 spectrophotometer (HITACHI, Japan). Time-resolved fluorescence decays were determined using a FLS 920 spectrometer (EI, UK) under excitation at 410 nm and the obtained lifetime decay curves were fitted using a double-exponential function Y(t) (eq. (1)) and the average lifetime was calculated according to eq. (2).
(1)

)
Here, A1 and A2 are the fractional contributions of time-resolved fluorescence decay lifetimes τ1 and τ2, respectively, and τ* refers to the average lifetime.

The quantum yield (QY) of the as-prepared Bio-CDs was measured by a well-established comparative method using quinine sulfate as standard. Typically, quinine sulfate with QY = 0.54 was dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 (refractive index of 1.33) while the Bio-CDs were dissolved into ethyl alcohol (refractive index of 1.36), their absorption values and fluorescent spectra were measured and the QY of Bio-CDs was calculated by the following equation:
(3)
Q
x
=
Q
s t
(
I
x
/
I
s t
)
(
A
s t
/
A
x
)
(
η
x
2
/
η
s t
2
)

Here, Q stands for QY. Grad refers to the gradient of the plot of integrated fluorescence peak area against absorbance. η represents the refractive index of the solvent. The subscripts x and st correspond to the Bio-CDs and quinine sulfate, respectively. In particular, the absorbance was adjusted to less than 0.05 to prevent re-absorption by both quinine sulfate and Bio-CDs solutions.

In our case, wood powders of palm veneers were used as a natural source of carbon to fabricate S, Cl doped biomass-based CDs in the presence of thionyl chloride as the dopant via a one-step hydrothermal treatment (Fig. 1 a). The obtained product was characterized by TEM to evidence our successfully synthesizing CDs and the TEM image reveals well-dispersed and quasi-spherical nanoparticles (Fig. 1b). Additionally, HRTEM image shown in Fig. 1c demonstrates that the Bio-CDs exhibited identical well-resolved lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing of 0.21 nm, which is close to that of the (100) facet of graphitic carbon, thus confirming the crystalline nature of Bio-CDs prepared via the hydrothermal method [31]. Moreover, the average diameter of Bio-CDs was calculated to be 3.54 ± 0.82 nm, and it is apparent that the Bio-CDs have a homogeneous size distribution, ranging from 2.5 to 5.5 nm (Fig. 1d).

The FT-IR measurement was performed to characterize the functional group of Bio-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1e, there are several common absorption peaks can be observed, including the stretching vibration bands generating from O−H (3403 cm−1), C−H (2929 cm−1), C=O (1711 cm−1), C=C (1631 cm−1), and the bending vibration of −COOH (1385 cm−1). Since the thionyl chloride participated in the Bio-CDs synthesis reaction, it is worth noting that the emergence of some bands could be highly related to the S and Cl elements. Typically, the band at 2488 cm−1 indicates the formation of S−H, whereas the peak at 1068 cm−1 corresponds to the C=S vibration [32]. The absorption peak at 1174 cm−1 can be attributed to the C−S bonds [33]. The weak peak at the wavenumber of 1010 cm−1 associates with C−S−C [34]. The peak at 1105 cm−1 could originate from the C−Cl bonds in chlorinated aromatic hydrocarbons, while the peaks at the wavenumber around 500-900 cm−1 are the characteristic C−Cl bonds that could be contained in chlorinated aliphatic hydrocarbons [35,36].

The XPS analyses were performed to further understand the element composition and valence state of CDs. As shown in Fig. 1f, two prominent features of the C 1s (285.7 eV) and O 1s (531.7 eV) peaks accompanied by three small peaks of S 2p (164.7 eV), Cl 2p (199.7 eV) and S 2s (229.7 eV) can be clearly observed in the full-scan survey of Bio-CDs. The relative atomic concentration of S, Cl, C, and O elements were determined to be 10.02%, 4.3%, 42.20% and 43.48%, respectively, thus indicating that S and Cl originating from thionyl chloride were introduced into the framework or surface composition of Bio-CDs, which is in accordance with the information of FT-IR spectrum. Furthermore, the high-resolution spectra of C 1s, O 1s, S 2p and Cl 2p of the prepared Bio-CDs were deconvolved and the obtained results are shown in Fig. 1g–i. The high-resolved C 1s XPS spectrum can be fitted into four peaks, which originate from C−C/C=C (284.3eV), C−S (285.6eV), C−O (286.7 eV) and O−C=O (288.1 eV) [37,38], respectively. The O 1s spectrum can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 531.5 and 533.0 eV, which represent O 1s states in C=O and C−OH/C−O−C [39]. Four distinct components centered at 164.7, 167.3, 168.3 and 169.5 eV in the S 2p spectrum manifest the presence of −C−S− bond and −C−SO x − (x = 2, 3, 4) species, respectively [40]. The peaks appeared at 199.7 and 201.3 eV were the binding energies of C−Cl and Cl−C=O, suggesting the covalent bonding of the chlorine atoms to the carbon skeleton [41]. The elemental species within the nanostructure of Bio-CDs were also extracted on the basis of XPS analysis, as shown in the Table 1 . The C=C/C−C moieties showed relatively low content with respect to most reported CDs so far [42], which indicates that a great number of incomplete sp2-hybridized carbon frameworks with carbon vacancy were formed in the carbogenic cores. In contrast, the relative content of heteroatom-related chemical species, such as C−N, C−S, C−Cl et al., was at a high level in our case, demonstrating the efficient heteroatom doping effect of thionyl chloride. Further, given these plentiful surface-contained functional groups, we reason that the heteroatom doping took dominant place in tailoring the photo-electronic properties. In addition, it is apparent that a great number of C−OH and C−O−C groups were attached on the surfaces of Bio-CDs as the reaction proceed, such functionalization contributed to energy level splitting and thus resulted in the excitation-dependent PL behaviors [43,44]. In light of aforementioned information, the Bio-CDs were confirmed to be well passivated with multiple functional groups on their surface, which is advantageous for the dispersion of CDs in aqueous solution. In particular, the S and Cl-containing groups could be prone to induce extra electronic transition channels within the structure of Bio-CDs and endowed these nanostructures with photocatalytic properties.

For fluorescent carbon materials, excitation-dependent luminescence behavior is a common feature. As shown in Fig. 2 a, accompanying the redshift of the excitation wavelength ranging from 300 nm to 380 nm, the peaks of emission shift to long wavelengths gradually. The strongest PL emission peaks appeared at around 425 nm when the Bio-CDs was excited at 340 nm (Fig. 2b). According to the information obtained from FT-IR spectrum and XPS-peak-differencing analysis, the surface of Bio-CDs was decorated with various functional groups, which favors the formation of different surface-defect states. These inhomogeneous defects are likely to generate multiple luminescent centers within the structures of Bio-CDs, and thus dominate PL emission with excitation-dependent behavior [45,46].

As shown in Fig. 2c inset, the diluted solution of Bio-CDs was pale yellow and transparent in daylight, while it emitted a blue fluorescence under the illumination of 365 nm UV light. The results of UV–vis absorption spectra and PLE spectra of Bio-CDs aqueous solution at room temperature are shown in the Fig. 2c. The two absorption shoulders located at 220 nm and 284 nm should be associated with two kinds of electron transitions. The former belongs to π‒π* transition within the core part of Bio-CDs, implying the formation of conjugated skeletons. The latter could be attributed to the energy trap derived from the heteroatom-passivated surface state or the n‒π* transition generating from C=O moieties [47,48]. Additionally, the PLE spectrum which was collected at the maximum luminescence showed two clear peaks at 276 nm and 345 nm. Notably, the peak of excitation spectrum was overlapped with the absorption band partially, thus confirming that the PL emission of Bio-CDs is mainly related to the trapping of excited state energy of the surface functional groups [49,50]. Besides, the fluorescence QY of Bio-CDs was determined as 0.9% at the corresponding optimal excitation, the main possible reason for this relatively low QY can be assigned to the self-absorption behavior that manifest as the overlap of excitation spectrum and absorption curve in our case.

Additionally, the PL lifetime decay of Bio-CDs was monitored at 340 nm and shown in Fig. 2d. The average lifetime of Bio-CDs was calculated as 4.53 ns, such a short lifetime indicates that the possible luminescent mechanism is the radioactive recombination nature of excitations [18]. The decay represents a biexponential fitting curve with short lifetimes (τ1) of 1.42 ns and long lifetimes (τ2) of 10.25 ns, and the components τ1 shared as high as 93%, indicating that the radiative electron transition mainly occurred within the carbogenic cores which was doped with S- and Cl-related chemical species [51].

For better understanding the possible mechanism for the synthesis of Bio-CDs, the control experiment was carried out under same condition, in which the palm powders were used as single precursor without doping of thionyl chloride. As a result, the synthesized product subjected to aggregation severely and showed no fluorescence under 365 nm excitation even after ultrasonic dispersion. Thus, the thionyl chloride play a key role in serving as dopant and forming individual nanoparticles during the CDs synthesis procedure. Typically, thionyl chloride prone to suffer thermal decomposition, and thus leading to the generation of acidic gas in the reaction system, which further results in the increase of hydrothermal pressure and the decrease of pH within the surroundings of starting materials. Indeed, the higher pressure, which together with acidic condition could facilitate the dehydration of complicated carbon-based molecules contained in palm powders and then enhanced the degree of carbonization of the formed molecular fragments or soluble polymers. Further, since the thionyl chloride helps to generate abundant chemical compounds in the synthetic reaction, the Bio-CDs surfaces were more likely to attach multiple functional groups after the reaction completed.

After adding the Bio-CDs, the dye molecules were gradually degraded under an artificial light. The relative changes in the concentration of the RhB and MB with a function of time were plotted. The results shown in Fig. 3 a-b demonstrate the degradation of RhB and MB in the absence and presence of Bio-CDs under different irradiation conditions (in darkness or visible light) within 45 min. It can be observed that only slight RhB and MB can be decomposed in the absence of Bio-CDs under the visible light. When the mixed solutions containing the Bio-CDs and dye molecules were exposed to visible light, the degradation of the RhB was nearly 71.7%, while the MB was almost decomposed completely (ca. 94.2%). The inserts also confirmed that the origin color of dye molecules disappeared under irradiation. As a control, the mixed solutions had nearly no degradation effects in the darkness. Meanwhile, the photocatalytic degradation kinetics of RhB and MB were investigated and the rate constant (k) values were calculated based on the first-order rate equation ln(Ct/C0) = −kt [52]. It can be found that the rate constant of the degradation of RhB and MB were 0.027 and 0.064, respectively, which were much higher than that of bare RhB and MB under visible light (Fig. 3c-d). By comparing the above results, the Bio-CDs had significant influence on the photodegradation of dye molecules under irradiation with the visible light.

The plausible photoreaction mechanism for the photodegradation is schematically shown in Fig. 4 . As well known, fluorescent CDs synthesized following bottom-up route along with heteroatom doping commonly behaves a broad emission and excitation-dependent emission phenomenon, such behavior occurs mainly due to the various energy levels associated with different defect states induced by O-containing groups and heteroatom doping effect. In addition, based on the first-principles calculations by Yan et al., level-split within the n–π* gap depends on the contents of C−OH and C−O−C groups, which provide important basis for the large shifting of emission arising from Bio-CDs [43]. Further, although all defect states are capable of trapping photoexcited electrons, the efficient radiative recombination within various defect states could be different. In our case, several distinct defect states induced by S and Cl-related groups can be generated, in which the electrons could hardly transition back to the ground state through different radiative recombination paths, thus leading to the efficient separation of electrons and holes [53]. As a proof, the relatively low QY of Bio-CDs is interpreted to be the fact that part of the photoexcited electrons were captured by the S and Cl-induced defect states, which lead to the degradation of PL.

Based on the above-mentioned deduction, electron-hole pair combination within Bio-CDs will be retarded to some extent in visible light, thus leading to the increase of free electrons, and the Bio-CDs surfaces with increased free electrons could react with the adsorbed oxidants (usually O2) to generate active oxygen radicals. Also the residual holes can be able to oxidize surface-absorbed hydroxyl groups and water to produce hydroxyl radicals. As a result, the hydroxyl radicals and holes generated on the surface of Bio-CDs have capacity to react with the dye molecules, which result in the decomposition of dye molecules into smaller hydrocarbons [54,55]. Moreover, it is note that the small sized Bio-CDs, which are featured with large surface area, have slight probability to absorb polluted dyes before irradiation with visible light but hardly losing their catalytic ability [56]. Therefore, this work has opened a new way to fabricate a photocatalyst which has a certain of surface adsorption and superior performance in the photodegradation of dye molecules.

In summary, we reported a kind of CDs with the excellent photocatalytic property successfully. Wood powders of palm veneers were used as a natural source of carbon to fabricate Bio-CDs in the presence of thionyl chloride as the dopant via a one-step hydrothermal treatment. The as-prepared Bio-CDs doped with S and Cl were mono-disperse with an average diameter of about 3.54 nm, and displayed excitation-dependent emission behavior. Furthermore, the most remarkable property of Bio-CDs is the high photocatalytic activity under visible light. We speculated that the Bio-CDs can be excited under visible light to generate efficient charge separation, the electrons and holes trapped at various surface defect sites of the nanoparticles can react with the adsorbed oxidants/reducers on the surface of Bio-CDs, which resulted in the production of active radicals that is beneficial for the degradation of dye molecules. Such a type of CDs with excellent metal-free photocatalytic property is a hopeful candidate as photocatalysts for the degradation of organic pollutants in the field of environmental science.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There is no conflicts of interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 31870549), the Jiangsu Nature Science Foundation (BK20161524), the Program for 333 Talents Project in Jiangsu Province (Grant No. BRA2016381), the Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (KYCX17_839), the Advanced Analysis and Testing Center of Nanjing Forestry University.

J. Am. Chem. Soc.

Nanoscale
